flashcards.front: What is Process Scheduling in Operating Systems?
flashcards.back: Process scheduling is the mechanism by which an operating system decides which process to execute on the CPU at any given time, and for how long, among multiple competing processes in memory.
A scheduler is a system software component that selects the next process to be admitted to the CPU from the ready queue based on a specific scheduling algorithm.

The dispatcher is the module that gives control of the CPU to the process selected by the short-term scheduler. It handles context switching, switching to user mode, and jumping to the proper location in the program.

flashcards.front: What are the three types of schedulers in OS?
flashcards.back: Long-term scheduler (admission scheduler) selects processes from job pool to ready queue. Short-term scheduler (CPU scheduler) selects from ready queue to CPU. Medium-term scheduler temporarily removes processes from memory.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of Long-term Scheduler?
flashcards.back: The long-term scheduler controls the degree of multiprogramming by deciding how many processes to admit to the ready queue. It balances I/O-bound and CPU-bound processes for efficient system performance.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of Short-term Scheduler?
flashcards.back: The short-term scheduler, also called the CPU scheduler, selects which ready process to execute next on the CPU. It is invoked frequently (every few milliseconds) and must be fast.
Context switching is the process of saving the state (context) of a currently running process and restoring the state of another process when switching the CPU between them.
Dispatch latency is the time taken by the dispatcher to stop one process and start another running. It includes context switching time and switching to user mode.

flashcards.front: What are Scheduling Criteria for evaluating algorithms?
flashcards.back: CPU Utilization (keep CPU busy), Throughput (processes completed per unit time), Turnaround Time (submission to completion), Waiting Time (time in ready queue), Response Time (first response).
First-Come-First-Served (FCFS) is a non-preemptive scheduling algorithm where the process that arrives first gets CPU first. It is simple but can cause the convoy effect.
Shortest Job First (SJF) selects the process with the smallest burst time. It can be preemptive (SRTF) or non-preemptive. It minimizes average waiting time but requires knowledge of burst times.
Round Robin is a preemptive scheduling algorithm that assigns a fixed time quantum to each process in a cyclic manner. All processes get equal CPU time slices.
Priority scheduling assigns a priority number to each process. The CPU is allocated to the process with highest priority. It can be preemptive or non-preemptive and may suffer from starvation.
Multilevel queue scheduling partitions the ready queue into separate queues, each with its own scheduling algorithm (e.g., system processes, interactive processes, batch processes).

flashcards.front: What is Multilevel Feedback Queue Scheduling?
flashcards.back: Multilevel Feedback Queue allows processes to move between queues. A process that uses too much CPU can be moved to a lower-priority queue, while I/O-bound processes stay in higher queues.
